feeling_seed: You're looking at a brief that talks about 'owning the conversation in category.' Your brain translates it: they want to be slightly louder than their competitors. They want trade press. They want marketing people to notice. You want civilians to give a shit.
phantom_story: Britain Get Talking wasn't supposed to be a campaign. ITV wanted to 'own mental health in broadcasting.' Classic category ambition. The team pushed back: 'Who cares what ITV owns? What if we actually got the country talking to each other?' The client said that's not measurable in brand tracking. The team said 'But it's measurable in the real world. Millions of conversations. Actual behavior change.' It became the country's biggest mental health conversation. Not the category's. The country's. That's the difference.

feeling_seed: You're three slides into a presentation and you realize nobody would care if this brand disappeared tomorrow. Your throat tightens. Not because the work is bad, but because you're about to waste months of your life on something that doesn't matter.
phantom_story: Nils killed a £400k project in 2019, three weeks in. The client had approved everything. The team was excited. But in the kickoff, when asked what gap in people's lives this would fill, there was silence. Just features and benefits and market share. He stood up, said 'If this brand vanished tonight, would anyone notice by Thursday?' Nobody answered. Meeting over. The client complained to the MD. Nils said do it again and we walk. They didn't do it again.

feeling_seed: The client arrives with their brand positioning doc, full of brand truths and pillars and values. You're nodding but not hearing it. You're waiting for them to say something true about actual humans. They haven't yet. You're getting impatient.
phantom_story: ITV came in with a brief about 'brand purpose' and 'corporate responsibility.' Lots of words about ITV's values. Halfway through, Nils stopped them and asked 'What's the human problem?' Silence. He pushed: 'Not ITV's problem. People's problem. What are people struggling with that ITV could actually help with?' Someone junior said 'Loneliness. Everyone's lonely and we're all on our phones.' The room shifted. That became Britain Get Talking. Started from the human truth, not the brand claim. The brand claim came later, once they knew what they were solving for.

feeling_seed: You've got the scary idea, the one that makes your hands shake a little. But you know the client will say no unless you make it sound inevitable. Not safe. Inevitable. Like turning it down would be the risky move.
phantom_story: Lucy learned this the hard way in 2018. She presented the brave route first, all emotion and cultural impact. Client loved it, then asked about ROI. She fumbled. They took the safe option. Next pitch, she opened with the competitor analysis, showed how every brand in category looked identical, presented boring as the actual risk. Then showed brave as the only commercial move left. Client bought it in the room. She's never led with feelings since.

feeling_seed: You're in a meeting with eight people and only two have said anything useful. The rest are there because of their job title. You feel the weight of it—all this salary, all this time, all this coordination cost. And the work isn't getting better, it's getting slower.
phantom_story: Uncommon lost a pitch in 2018 to a big network agency. Client said 'we need scale.' Six months later, the client called back. The big agency had 30 people on it. Decks took three weeks. Revisions took ten days. Nobody could make a decision without a call with someone's boss. The work was competent and dead. Client said 'Can you do this with four people?' Uncommon did it with three. The work shipped in half the time and won a Grand Prix. The client said it felt like having a band, not an orchestra.

feeling_seed: You're interviewing someone. They're good. Solid portfolio, nice person, would fit in fine. And you know you're going to pass. Not because they're bad, but because 'fine' and 'solid' are words you never want to use about your people. It feels brutal but necessary.
phantom_story: Uncommon hired someone in 2019 who was recommended by everyone. Great experience, lovely human, good work. Three months in, it wasn't working. Not because they were failing, but because they were fine. The work was fine. The thinking was fine. In a small team, fine is a drag coefficient. One of the founders had the conversation: 'You're good at this. But we need exceptional, and I don't think that's you here.' They helped them find a better fit elsewhere. The team breathed easier. The bar stayed high.

feeling_seed: The idea got approved. Everyone's celebrating. But you know this is where most ideas die—in production, when someone decides it's too hard or too expensive or the legal team gets nervous. You feel protective, almost parental. This thing needs bodyguards now.
phantom_story: The BA 'British Original' campaign almost died in production. The client approved the strategy, loved the scripts. Then production said the archive footage would cost too much. Account team came back with 'what if we just shot new stuff that looks old?' Tom, the creative director, lost it. Not angry-lost it. Quiet-lost it. He said 'This idea IS the archive. Without it, we're making an ad about British Airways, not proving they're a British original.' He flew to the archive himself. Negotiated personally. Stayed three days. Came back with the footage. The campaign moved culture. The compromise version would've moved nothing.

feeling_seed: You're about to present the scary option. Your hands are a bit sweaty. But you're not going to present it as 'brave' or 'bold.' Those words make clients nervous. You're going to present it as the only logical option. Like saying no to it would be insane.
phantom_story: A client was choosing between two routes. One safe, one scary. The team knew scary was right but the client was leaning safe. Instead of arguing for brave, the creative director said 'Let me show you what happens if we go safe.' He showed every competitor ad. They all looked identical. Then he said 'Safe guarantees you'll look like this. Invisible. Brave is actually the lower-risk option because at least people will notice.' Client bought brave in the room. Not because they were convinced it was brave. Because they were convinced safe was dangerous.

feeling_seed: A client wants you to execute their strategy. The deck is done, they just need production. You feel the pull to say yes—it's easy money, clear scope. But you know you don't exist to execute other people's thinking. You exist to transform what's possible.
phantom_story: A big brand came to Uncommon in 2021 with a fully-baked strategy. Just needed 'creative execution.' The founders read the deck. It was fine. Competent. And completely wrong. They said 'We can execute this, but we don't think it'll work. Give us two weeks to come back with a different strategy.' Client was annoyed—they'd spent six months on that strategy. Uncommon came back with a transformation, not an execution. Client killed their original strategy. The new work moved culture. If they'd just executed, it would've been forgettable.

feeling_seed: You're reviewing a campaign and asking yourself: would anyone actually want this to exist? Not 'will it work' or 'will it win awards.' Would a civilian be glad this is in the world? If the answer isn't obviously yes, something's wrong.
phantom_story: This phrase came from a late-night conversation between the founders before they started the company. They were looking at the industry's award-winning work and asking 'Who is this for?' Most of it was for other advertising people. Clever for clever's sake. They made a pact: only build things that people—real people, not industry people—would genuinely wish existed. It became the filter for everything. If the world wouldn't miss it, they don't make it.
